name: Numerical Methods
summary: Computational techniques for solving physics problems that have no closed-form analytical solution — the bread and butter of computational physics.
whenToUseIt: Use numerical methods when analytical solutions are impossible or impractical, when dealing with complex geometries, nonlinear equations, many-body systems, or when you need to simulate time evolution.
steps: Formulate the physics problem as a mathematical equation (ODE, PDE, integral, etc.)
Discretise the problem — replace continuous variables with discrete grid points or time steps
Choose an appropriate algorithm (Euler, Runge-Kutta, finite element, Monte Carlo, etc.)
Implement the algorithm and validate against known analytical solutions
Analyse convergence, stability, and numerical errors
example: Simulating the three-body problem (three stars orbiting each other) — there is no general analytical solution. Using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta integrator, you can compute the orbits numerically and discover chaotic behaviour where tiny changes in initial conditions lead to wildly different outcomes.
